images.caption: Figure 1:Pearsonrrbetween activation similarity and behavioral CV across steps and layers (n=99n=99; one question excluded at step 4 as all its runs terminated earlier). Gold borders indicatep<0.05p<0.05. The signal concentrates at step 4 across layers 32–80.

Figure 2:Activation similarity at step 4 by commitment category. Committed-wrong and committed-correct questions overlap, while uncommitted-wrong questions trend lower. The diagnostic tracks whether the agent has settled, not whether it is right.
Figure 3:Layer-wise correlation (activation similarity vs. CV) at step 4 across three models, layers normalized to proportional depth. Gold borders:p<0.05p<0.05.

Figure 4:The intervention dissociates representation from token count.(A)Activation similarity rises Control→\toFiller→\toCommitment.(B)Behavioral variance falls only under Commitment; the token-matched Filler raises similarity but worsens CV. Values from Table1.
Figure 5:Correlation between activation similarity and CV at layer 40 across steps. The consistency signal peaks at step 4.

Figure 6:Step progression of the commitment signal across three models (each at its peak layer: Llama L40, Qwen L64, Phi-3 L16). Both 70B models peak at step 4; Phi-3 (14B) peaks at step 5, suggesting smaller models need one additional evidence-gathering step. Gold borders:p<0.05p<0.05.

Figure 7:Step-4 layer-wise correlation between activation similarity and behavioral CV, split by difficulty (n=100n{=}100).Easyquestions (right) show a strong negative correlation at every non-embedding layer;hardquestions (left) show essentially no linear relationship, as predicted when committed-wrong states dominate.p∗<0.05{}^{*}p<0.05.
Figure 8:t-SNE projection of step-4 hidden states colored by commitment category. Committed-correct (green) and committed-wrong (red) overlap; uncommitted-wrong (orange) is more dispersed.

header: Appendix HBaseline predictors of behavioral CV
Appendix IPrototype distance signal for hard questions

images.caption: Figure 9:Hard-question prototype signal: cosine similarity to hard-question centroid vs. behavioral CV (step 4, layer 32). Questions further from the centroid are more consistent (r=0.43r=0.43, perm.p=0.004p=0.004).

header: Appendix JCommitment as a linear direction

images.caption: Figure 10:Projection of per-question mean hidden states onto𝐯commit\mathbf{v}_{\mathrm{commit}}vs. behavioral CV. Easy questions (blue) cluster at high projection / low CV; hard (orange) spread across the axis. Llama layer 40, step 4.
Figure 11:ROC curves for consistency prediction (quintile labeling). Hidden-state features (solid) outperform pre-completion surface baselines (dashed).
Figure 12:Consistency-prediction AUROC vs. number of runs. Atk=3k{=}3, AUROC is0.81±0.070.81\pm 0.07. Shaded: 95% bootstrap CI.

Figure 13:StrategyQA step×\timeslayer correlation heatmap (Llama-3.1-70B,n=50n{=}50). The signal concentrates at step 3, one step earlier than HotpotQA’s step 4 peak. All layers 8–80 at step 3 show|r|>0.72|r|>0.72(p<10−8p<10^{-8}).

Figure 14:Activation similarity (layer 40) across agent steps for the three intervention conditions (n=89n{=}89matched questions). Conditions are identical through step 3 (intervention point, dashed line), then diverge at step 4: commitment highest (0.995), filler intermediate (0.979), control lowest (0.922).

Figure 15:Question-level CV reduction (xx) vs. accuracy change (yy) under commitment (n=100n{=}100). The negative correlation (r=−0.32r=-0.32,p=.001p=.001) shows commitment amplifies the model’s existing trajectory: lower variance without a systematic accuracy gain.
